kanji.meaningMnemonic: You use a <radical>bamboo</radical> <radical>saw</radical> to cut down <radical>tree</radical>s to <kanji>construct</kanji> and <kanji>build</kanji> a building. The bamboo saw was crafted by a master bamboosmith, and cuts through trees with ease so you can quickly construct this new building.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine yourself working the bamboo saw. It's made of bamboo, but its sharp edge glistens with a mysterious aura as you slice through tree after tree. Feel how unnaturally easy it is to cut down piles of logs to build with.
kanji.readingMnemonic: As you <kanji>construct</kanji> and <kanji>build</kanji> this building, you hear a strange cheeping noise. Suddenly a <reading>chick</reading> (ちく) pops out of one of the logs you are building with. Then another, and another. You're surrounded by chicks! Is this because you used the magical(?) bamboo saw??
kanji.readingHint: Imagine trying to construct the rest of this building covered in baby chicks. They're very cute, but this is dangerous! You have to build very carefully, so you don't accidentally hurt any of them.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You should <radical>say</radical> "<radical>peace</radical>" before you <kanji>evaluate</kanji> a situation. That way everyone will know where you’re coming from. If you’re honest and say "peace" before you evaluate a situation out loud, everyone will know where you stand.
kanji.meaningHint: What situation would you like to evaluate? Be sure to say "peace" before doing so. Imagine yourself doing this out loud and think of what you would say.
kanji.readingMnemonic: When you say "peace," you end up making a really bad pun and you hear somebody say “<reading>Heyo</reading>!” (ひょう) You look at the people around you and try to <kanji>evaluate</kanji> who said heyo, but you can’t tell. You start talking again when somebody exclaims “That’s what she said” and another person shouts “Heyo!” once again, but you still can’t evaluate who’s saying these things.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine yourself trying to explain a situation you’re about to evaluate and your audience keeps shouting “Heyo!” every other sentence. Imagine how frustrating that would be. Get angry at your rude audience and how much they shout “Heyo.”

rawData.meaning_hint: What situation would you like to evaluate? Be sure to say "peace" before doing so. Imagine yourself doing this out loud and think of what you would say.

You start talking again when somebody exclaims “That’s what she said” and another person shouts “Heyo!” once again, but you still can’t evaluate who’s saying these things.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You'll be put in a <radical>net</radical> if you try to <radical>say</radical> things with a <radical>knife</radical> — that's your <kanji>penalty</kanji>. You can't just go up to other people and say things with a knife out. That's why the police have to put you in a net, as a penalty.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine getting so mad that you use a knife to say what you're trying to say. A net falls over you and pulls you down. The knife goes flying. This is your penalty for acting like a crazy person.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Your <kanji>penalty</kanji> for breaking the law isn't over. Now that you're stuck in a net, you can't fight back when they hit you with <reading>bats</reading> (ばつ).
kanji.readingHint: If you don't want to be punished with bats, just skip the whole knife thing. Use your words to say what you mean, mmkay?

kanji.meaningMnemonic: They <radical>say</radical> <radical>yoga</radical> is the <radical>correct</radical> way to go through <kanji>birth</kanji>. Birth isn't easy, but people keep telling you that yoga is the right way to prepare and actually do it.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture multiple people you know saying you should do yoga if you plan to give birth. It isn't a question of whether you want to, it's the correct way to do it. So you should.
kanji.readingMnemonic: To prepare for giving <kanji>birth</kanji> you end up doing so much yoga outside that you get a really deep <reading>tan</reading> (たん).
kanji.readingHint: Hopefully having a tan doesn't make birth harder, since you've worked so hard to do this the correct way with so much yoga.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The light of the <radical>moon</radical> is shining on a <radical>guard</radical> who’s been on duty all night. In fact, the moon light is making him so warm, he’s trying to <kanji>undress</kanji> and <kanji>escape</kanji>! Somebody stop him!
kanji.meaningHint: Picture the guard being so warm from the moonlight that he’s trying to simultaneously undress and escape. You can’t just let him leave his post!
kanji.readingMnemonic: You’re unable to stop the guard from <kanji>undress</kanji>ing and making his <kanji>escape</kanji>, so you decide to start throwing some <reading>darts</reading> (だつ) at him. Perhaps that’ll make him stop.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine yourself desperately throwing darts at the undressing, escaping guard, trying to make him stop. Or perhaps trying to pin his clothes to his body using the darts. Anything to stop this madness!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: While riding on your <radical>scooter</radical>, you grow a <radical>mustache</radical> on top of the <radical>mustache</radical> you already have in order to <kanji>surpass</kanji> the people around you. You're in a scooter race and your mustache is the only thing keeping you in the lead, but someone else has a mustache too! So you grow another mustache to surpass them once again!
kanji.meaningHint: Picture yourself running on a scooter with a bunch of other people and closing your eyes tight, willing another mustache to sprout out of your face to surpass them all.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Suddenly, something else manages to <kanji>surpass</kanji> you all. It's a <reading>ca</reading>r (か)! A car with two mustaches is also riding on a scooter and going way faster than you.
kanji.readingHint: Watch helplessly as the car surpasses every single one of your scooters. Both of your mustaches droop in shame.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Can you look at the <radical>dirt</radical> and know which <radical>direction</radical> you need to go? Then you might be a <kanji>monk</kanji>. Monks have amazing skills like that. And I'm sure you've seen monks wandering around in lots of media. They don't need maps or smartphones. They just look at the dirt and let the earth guide them to where they need to be.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture a monk walking through a large field in the middle of nowhere. He knows where to go, he's not afraid. The dirt will lead him in the right direction.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You want to know the <kanji>monk</kanji>'s trick so you hide behind him for a while as he walks. Finally the monk stops, looks around, takes out a <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball and starts bowling in the dirt. The bowling ball rolls off, the monk follows it, and then heads off in that direction.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine a monk bowling in the middle of the road. Imagine your shock at how well he handles the ball. Also, imagine how heavy it must be for a monk to carry a bowling ball everywhere!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: From the <radical>roof</radical>, an <radical>icicle</radical> falls and hits <radical>Wolverine</radical> in the <radical>forehead</radical>, and he has to go over to the <radical>stool</radical> to <kanji>lie down</kanji>. And eventually he goes to <kanji>sleep</kanji>.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture this entire scene. The icicle falls in slow motion from the roof. It hits wolverine right in the middle of his forehead. He goes down. He has to lie down. He has to sleep.
kanji.readingMnemonic: While wolverine <kanji>sleep</kanji>s, he makes a horrible <reading>neigh</reading>ing (ね) sound. It's like there's a giant horse in his mouth screaming to get out! NEIGH NEIGH!
kanji.readingHint: Wolverine might have a concussion that's making him neigh in his sleep. I guess it's good that he's lying down, but I'm pretty sure you're not supposed to sleep... Maybe you should wake him up so the neighing will stop—I mean, so he'll be okay!

kanji.readingMnemonic: What god does this <kanji>Shinto shrine</kanji> hold? You see that "It's a-<reading>me-ya</reading> (みや)! (Mario!)"

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You <radical>kiss</radical> <kanji>each</kanji> and <kanji>every</kanji> one of your best pals to show them how much you love and care for them. You kiss each friend right on the mouth.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine kissing each of your best friends on the mouth. Does this make you uncomfortable? Excited? Both? Feel that feeling.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Actually, <kanji>each</kanji> and <kanji>every</kanji> one of your best pals is of the <reading>cock</reading> (かく) variety. By that, I mean each of them is a rooster. Imagine kissing each cock on its beak.
kanji.readingHint: Kissing the cock beak is kind of a strange experience. Feel the feathers in your hands as you steady the cock to kiss it. Cock-a-doodle-doo!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>cheap</radical> <radical>tree</radical> has a <kanji>plan</kanji>. A plan to stay cheap.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture a tree coming up with some elaborate plans to make sure they can spend as little money as possible.
kanji.readingMnemonic: The cheap tree's <kanji>plan</kanji> involves only buying <reading>an</reading>chovies (あん) and surviving on them. Good exposure to the sun and fresh water is too expensive. The tree plans to save money by eating anchovies.
kanji.readingHint: You're not sure about this anchovy plan. Are you cheap enough to choose a life of eating only anchovies?

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You need your <radical>net</radical> to be <radical>straight</radical> before you <kanji>put</kanji> it on the ground and try to lure something into it. You put your net there to catch something, but if it's twisted or tangled, it won't be very effective.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine that some wild animal wanders into your net. Yes! You knew putting the net there was a good idea. But wait… Oh no, it escaped! It turns out your net wasn't straight when you put it there.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Then you decide to <kanji>put</kanji> <reading>chee</reading>se (ち) in your net. With a perfectly straight net and the cheese you put down as bait, there’s no way this fails. Success is all about how you put the net down and the cheese you put in it.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine the smell of the cheese as you put it in the net. Maybe a sharp cheddar? The aroma really carries. All the better to attract more animals! Soon you’ll be putting your cheese-eating prey on the dinner table.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The <radical>womb</radical> is a <radical>circle</radical> of <radical>power</radical> where a <kanji>force</kanji> of nature is formed. The womb has the power to create life. Life is a force of nature, and the circle of life starts in the womb.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine yourself in the womb, the circle of power. You’re being born into the world. Feel the force of your mother all around you.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Now pretend you're the mother. You'll need all the <kanji>force</kanji> you can muster to bring that baby into the world, so you grab hold of your special <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) and slice it about in the air to give you the extra force you need.
kanji.readingHint: All that force works wonders, and when the baby's born you slice through the umbilical cord with your saber.

rawData.meaning_hint: Imagine yourself in the womb, the circle of power. You’re being born into the world. Feel the force of your mother all around you.
rawData.reading_hint: All that force works wonders, and when the baby's born you slice through the umbilical cord with your saber.
rawData.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>womb</radical> is a <radical>circle</radical> of <radical>power</radical> where a <kanji>force</kanji> of nature is formed. The womb has the power to create life. Life is a force of nature, and the circle of life starts in the womb.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>Viking</radical> who knows how to use a <radical>bathtub</radical> will have to <kanji>manage</kanji> all the baths for the other Vikings. Vikings aren't so great with bathing... if you can get one Viking to figure it out he has to manage the rest of them.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture a Viking trying to manage his Viking friends so they'll use the bathtub properly. He has to manage which people go in when, how hot or cold the water is, and provide all the soap. It's a very important job.
kanji.readingMnemonic: The poor Viking trying to <kanji>manage</kanji> all the others keeps having to confiscate <reading>a</reading>corns (えい). "You can't take baths in acorns and wood, it has to be water!" he shouts.
kanji.readingHint: Some of the Vikings managed to fill their bathtubs with acorns and are trying to rub themselves clean with them instead of water...

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You can use a <radical>jackhammer</radical> to <kanji>indicate</kanji> and <kanji>show</kanji> someone where the prize is buried. You buried the prize to keep it safe, but the jackhammer is great at unearthing it. Jackhammers do a great job and show where the treasure lies. They indicate quite clearly where one needs to go.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine using this jackhammer to show someone where the prize is buried. Feel the vibrations through your hands and the rest of your body.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Actually, you're unearthing <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ). He’s the treasure. For your soul. He’s here to show you that Jesus is still a hip and cool guy and he wants to <kanji>indicate</kanji> to you that having faith in Jesus and God and stuff is super rad. Jesus is all like, “Who has two thumbs and wants to <kanji>show</kanji> you the way of the Lord? This guy.”
kanji.readingHint: Picture Jesus getting all over the top and super preachy about his religious beliefs. He’s doing everything he can to indicate to the crowd that he’s the best, and show them how cool he is. Listen to Jesus preach to the crowd, desperate for their attention.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You take all of your <radical>thread</radical> and <radical>allocate</radical> them into one place to <kanji>unite</kanji> them. With all your thread united and wrapped together, you're ready to <kanji>rule</kanji> and <kanji>govern</kanji> the world!
kanji.meaningHint: Allocate all of your thread like you would your cash: by uniting it! Not at a bank, but in a big, intertwined lump. Now nobody can stop you and your big lump of thread!
kanji.readingMnemonic: You're busy trying to <kanji>unite</kanji> all of your thread, twisting and tying and wrapping, and suddenly you look at the ball in your hands and see <reading>とう</reading>きょう. You've united this thread into the shape of とうきょう. There are even little pins for all the famous neighborhoods and tourist spots. This is strange, because you don't remember putting them there.
kanji.readingHint: Try to do this yourself at home. Unite all of your thread and poof! There's とうきょう!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Taking <radical>orders</radical> from a <radical>geoduck</radical> will place you into enemy <kanji>territory</kanji>. Geoducks make terrible commanders, so if you take orders from one, expect to end up in unknown territory, potentially even enemy territory. Be prepared.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine this geoduck shouting orders at you to march into enemy territory. See its gross body as a mouth forms and starts shouting out orders.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Not wanting to end up dead in enemy <kanji>territory</kanji>, you decide to escape this geoduck leader by getting on a <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat and rowing your way outta there. The geoduck commander orders you to halt your escape, but you still get in your row boat and row away.
kanji.readingHint: Feel yourself in the row boat as you imagine rowing away to safe territory. Hear the geoduck in the background shouting at you from shore, ordering you to return to land.

rawData.meaning_hint: Imagine this geoduck shouting orders at you to march into enemy territory. See its gross body as a mouth forms and starts shouting out orders.
rawData.reading_hint: Feel yourself in the row boat as you imagine rowing away to safe territory. Hear the geoduck in the background shouting at you from shore, ordering you to return to land.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: With a <radical>bamboo</radical> <radical>tree</radical> resting atop your <radical>head</radical>, you devise a <kanji>plan</kanji>. The plan is actually for getting the bamboo tree off of your head. You’re trapped under it.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine being trapped under this bamboo tree that is resting on your head. You’re stuck. Feel the weight of the bamboo tree pressing on your head as you try and devise a plan to get out from under it.
kanji.readingMnemonic: But why is this bamboo tree so heavy? You look to each end of the bamboo and realize that there is a <reading>sack</reading> (さく) tied on each end. Each sack seems to be filled with heavy rocks. Looks like someone made a <kanji>plan</kanji> to trap you here. Now you have to devise a plan to escape!
kanji.readingHint: Bamboo is usually kind of light, but this bamboo has a sack of rocks tied to each end making it heavy. Imagine yourself trying to remove this bamboo tree by pushing it up and away from you. It’s like doing a bench-press, bamboo style.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The only <radical>flowers</radical> that the <radical>moon</radical> <radical>gladiator</radical>s will <radical>water</radical> are <kanji>wisteria</kanji>.
kanji.meaningHint: The moon gladiators have a thing for wisterias.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Since they're on the moon, where did they get the <kanji>wisteria</kanji> from? Actually, from one of the points on earth that is nearest to the moon, Mt. <reading>Fuji</reading> (ふじ).
kanji.readingHint: Imagine them reaching down from the moon and plucking some wisteria up from Mt. Fuji then taking them back up to the moon. Now they water them.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: That <radical>scarecrow</radical> with the <radical>knife</radical> is the <kanji>vice</kanji> president. Yeah, I know how he looks, and I'm pretty sure he does some murdering on the <kanji>side</kanji>, but you didn't hear that from me.
kanji.meaningHint: I don't know how we managed to get a knife-wielding scarecrow as a vice president, there's a side of politics I don't get, I guess. I mean, just look at him up there. If something happens to our president we're stuck with that thing.
kanji.readingMnemonic: It's the <kanji>vice</kanji> president's turn to speak at the podium and he just clears his throat and yells, "<reading>F**k</reading> (ふく) you!" to the crowd, holding up his knife. They cheer.
kanji.readingHint: What's going on? Normally if a vice president yells f**k you there would be... I dunno... consequences? What's happening?!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: There's an <radical>arrow</radical> in a <radical>turkey</radical> that you can <radical>see</radical> in your <kanji>view</kanji>. The turkey seems to be viewing you too, from afar.
kanji.meaningHint: View the turkey as the turkey views you. View the arrow stuck in it too.
kanji.readingMnemonic: As you <kanji>view</kanji> the turkey, Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) suddenly comes into your view too. He towers over the injured turkey, leans down, and... pulls the arrow out!?
kanji.readingHint: As you view this strange scene, Genghis Khan turns and views you viewing him. He points two fingers to his eyes and then to you, and marches off into the woods. You also view the turkey running off in the other direction.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A great <radical>leader</radical> can look <radical>straight</radical> at something and know its <kanji>value</kanji>. That social media site? 44 billion dollars. That job position? 50k a year. A four-day work week? Um… sorry, no, there's no value there. See? Leaders can see straight to the heart of matters to grasp something's true value.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine how terrifying it is when your leader looks straight at YOU. Don't worry, though — you've got more value than you give yourself credit for, and your leader can surely see that.
kanji.readingMnemonic: One thing your leader knows has great <kanji>value</kanji> is <reading>chee</reading>se (ち). Say goodbye to your days of casually snacking on cheesy treats from the conbini, because your leader's decided the value of cheese is going up 1000%!
kanji.readingHint: Imagine the despair you feel walking down the cheese aisle at the store and seeing how one little pack of string cheese now costs the same as your monthly rent. Why did your leader have to spike the value of cheese like this?

rawData.meaning_hint: Imagine how terrifying it is when your leader looks straight at YOU. Don't worry, though — you've got more value than you give yourself credit for, and your leader can surely see that.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Your <radical>mouth</radical> is like an <radical>escalator</radical> you use to <kanji>suck</kanji> and <kanji>inhale</kanji> things into your body.
kanji.meaningHint: If you're ever having trouble remembering how to suck or inhale, just picture an actual escalator that leads into your mouth. That'll definitely help.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Use this mouth ability to <kanji>suck</kanji> <reading>cu</reading>cumbers (きゅう) into your mouth. Cucumbers are shaped like cylinders so you can suck them into your mouth like this. Just as nature intended.
kanji.readingHint: You can inhale a cucumber too, I suppose, but don't tell anyone I told you to.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Lying in the <radical>dirt</radical>, a <radical>drunkard</radical> puts her <radical>mouth</radical> to the <radical>ground</radical> to understand the <kanji>region</kanji>. She loves to know all the regional differences among wines and other alcohols, and to her there's no better way to do this than putting her mouth on the ground and literally tasting each region.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture this drunkard in the dirt, her mouth pressed to the ground. "Ahh," she says, "so this is the taste of the Bordeaux region." Then she has a glass of Bordeaux, and starts noting all its particular regional qualities.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Turns out she thinks everything from this <kanji>region</kanji> is <reading>icky</reading> (いき). In fact, to the drunkard, every region tastes icky and gross. You don't have the heart to tell her it's the dirt and ground, not the region itself, that tastes icky…
kanji.readingHint: Imagine her crying out, "Why are there such icky, earthy notes in all these regional wines?!" Feel the second-hand embarrassment for her. In her drunkenness she can't understand that putting her mouth on the ground makes everything taste icky, no matter what region it's from.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You're always comparing yourself to the <radical>next</radical> <radical>woman</radical> because you're obsessed with your <kanji>figure</kanji> and <kanji>appearance</kanji>. It's not healthy, but when physical <kanji>form</kanji> is the only thing that matters to you, comparing yourself to the next woman over becomes a matter of habit.
kanji.meaningHint: Try to picture this proverbial "next woman." Ugh, her figure is just perfect. And her appearance? So put-together.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Plot twist: when you're comparing yourself to the next woman, the <kanji>figure</kanji> and <kanji>appearance</kanji> you're picturing is actually that of a <reading>shee</reading>p (し). No wonder you're always dissatisfied with your appearance. You're not a sheep, you're a human!
kanji.readingHint: Imagine standing in front of the mirror, wishing desperately that you could see the figure of a sheep reflected back at you. What do you have to do to attain that round, fluffy appearance???

kanji.meaningMnemonic: If someone gets under the <radical>canopy</radical> over your <radical>heart</radical> you should <kanji>respond</kanji> to them.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You extend your <radical>fingers</radical> toward the <radical>sun</radical> from atop a <radical>coat rack</radical> because you are about to <kanji>present</kanji> your ideas to a crowd. You need to be striking an authoritative pose from an elevated position in order to <kanji>submit</kanji> your thoughts persuasively.
kanji.meaningHint: Extending your fingers upward makes you look like some sort of prophet, and sitting up on the coat rack puts you head and shoulders above all your listeners. Well, go ahead: present your ideas. (You do have something to present, right?)
kanji.readingMnemonic: So, what are you going to <kanji>present</kanji> to everyone? A slideshow about <reading>ta</reading>ter tots (てい). You made a little PowerPoint to present your personal ranking of the best tater tots you've ever eaten — with photos, of course.
kanji.readingHint: At the end of your presentation, you ask everyone to submit a feedback questionnaire, and you present free tater tot samples to everyone who does. What a fun presentation.

kanji.meaningMnemonic:
You use your <radical>fingers</radical> in the game of <radical>football</radical> to <kanji>aid</kanji> and <kanji>assist</kanji> the other players. And when you smash your fingers in football, you need to get some aid yourself.

If you're from the UK, just imagine someone kicking or stomping on your fingers instead.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture yourself playing football. You catch the ball with your fingers and then assist your team by running and smashing your body into the enemy team. But in the process you smash your fingers... so you need to go to the aid tent.
kanji.readingMnemonic: When you need <kanji>aid</kanji> for your football wrecked fingers you can't help but feel like it's the <reading>en</reading>d (えん) for you. Your fingers hurt so much, you don't even want the nice nurses to aid you.
kanji.readingHint: You can't assist your team anymore because you got hurt. This isn't fair! It's the end. The end of your life and your career. Boo hoo hoo!

If you're from the UK, just imagine someone kicking or stomping on your fingers instead.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: See all those <radical>icicle</radical>s to the <radical>dog</radical>'s left? Good thing they all fell on his left, or he would've been in a terrible <kanji>condition</kanji>.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture a bunch of big, sharp icicles all falling down directly next to this dog. Now imagine the condition he would've been in, had they all fallen directly on top of him. Poor little thing.
kanji.readingMnemonic: What saved the dog's <kanji>condition</kanji> was none other than <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the local farmhand. He constantly keeps an eye on the dog's condition, including any dangerously sharp icicles that could descend on him.
kanji.readingHint: Good guy Joe, always worrying about his dog's condition. Everyone needs a farmhand like Joe.

rawData.meaning_hint: Picture a bunch of big, sharp icicles all falling down directly next to this dog. Now imagine the condition he would've been in, had they all fallen directly on top of him. Poor little thing.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You have a great <radical>ability</radical> because your <radical>heart</radical> gives you the <kanji>appearance</kanji> of a superhuman. Your heart is really strong and powerful, fuelling your body to the best of its ability. All this together gives you the appearance of someone in top condition.
kanji.meaningHint: See yourself in this ideal appearance. Everyone loves to look at you because you look so awesome. Your heart has given you many great abilities. Feel strong and powerful in this body of yours.
kanji.readingMnemonic: While you may have the <kanji>appearance</kanji> of someone who is super strong, powerful, and awesome, you do have one shortcoming. You don’t know how to <reading>tie</reading> (たい) a tie. Actually, you don’t even know how to tie your shoes, let alone your tie. How embarrassing.
kanji.readingHint: Since you don’t know how to tie a tie or tie your shoes, you wear slip ons and clip ons. Hear the noise of Velcro being undone as you kick off your shoes and unclip your tie after a hard day’s work at the office.

rawData.meaning_hint: See yourself in this ideal appearance. Everyone loves to look at you because you look so awesome. Your heart has given you many great abilities. Feel strong and powerful in this body of yours.
rawData.reading_hint: Since you don’t know how to tie a tie or tie your shoes, you wear slip ons and clip ons. Hear the noise of Velcro being undone as you kick off your shoes and unclip your tie after a hard day’s work at the office.

Your heart is really strong and powerful, fuelling your body to the best of its ability. All this together gives you the appearance of someone in top condition.

You don’t know how to <reading>tie</reading> (たい) a tie. Actually, you don’t even know how to tie your shoes, let alone your tie. How embarrassing.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You use the <radical>power</radical> of your <radical>mouth</radical> to spit <radical>shellfish</radical> at people as a form of <kanji>congratulations</kanji>! In fact, you yell "CONGRATULATIONS!" as the shellfish come pouring out of your powerful mouth.
kanji.meaningHint: Shellfish were once used as currency, so this totally makes sense. Just think of the shellfish as money coming out of your mouth as you say your congratulations.
kanji.readingMnemonic: When you say <kanji>congratulations</kanji> like this, you look just like a <reading>ga</reading>rgoyle (が). You're crouching down, your mouth wide open, just like a stone gargoyle.
kanji.readingHint: The next time you're congratulating someone, assume the gargoyle stance and dump all of their congratulatory shellfish on them from your mouth.

rawData.meaning_hint: Shellfish were once used as currency, so this totally makes sense. Just think of the shellfish as money coming out of your mouth as you say your congratulations.
rawData.reading_hint: The next time you're congratulating someone, assume the gargoyle stance and dump all of their congratulatory shellfish on them from your mouth.
rawData.meaning_mnemonic: You use the <radical>power</radical> of your <radical>mouth</radical> to spit <radical>shellfish</radical> at people as a form of <kanji>congratulations</kanji>! In fact, you yell "CONGRATULATIONS!" as the shellfish come pouring out of your powerful mouth.
rawData.reading_mnemonic: When you say <kanji>congratulations</kanji> like this, you look just like a <reading>ga</reading>rgoyle (が). You're crouching down, your mouth wide open, just like a stone gargoyle.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: In the <radical>ground</radical> of the <radical>rice paddy</radical> is a <radical>treasure</radical>. As you pull it out of the mucky ground, you can feel your life force being <kanji>renew</kanji>ed.
kanji.meaningHint: Seems like the treasure has some magical ability to renew the cells in your body. Really try to imagine this feeling as you pull the treasure out of the rice paddy’s ground. Perhaps it feels just as refreshing as getting a full night’s sleep.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Just as you were getting accustomed to this life <kanji>renew</kanji>ing treasure, <reading>こう</reading>いち comes out of nowhere, stealing your treasure and running off with it.
kanji.readingHint: All you could hear was a sly “yoink!”, and suddenly your life renewing treasure was gone. God knows what things こういち will get up to with this life renewing treasure in his possession (but it sure won’t be anything good).

rawData.meaning_hint: Seems like the treasure has some magical ability to renew the cells in your body. Really try to imagine this feeling as you pull the treasure out of the rice paddy’s ground. Perhaps it feels just as refreshing as getting a full night’s sleep.
rawData.reading_hint: All you could hear was a sly “yoink!”, and suddenly your life renewing treasure was gone. God knows what things こういち will get up to with this life renewing treasure in his possession (but it sure won’t be anything good).

rawData.reading_mnemonic: Just as you were getting accustomed to this life <kanji>renew</kanji>ing treasure, <reading>こう</reading>いち comes out of nowhere, stealing your treasure and running off with it.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Inside the <radical>building</radical> is the <radical>Winter</radical> <radical>Cow</radical>. You need to see it, so you go inside. It is deep down in there, and you'll have to <kanji>descend</kanji> to reach it.
kanji.meaningHint: Focus on descending the stairs. There are many signs that say "To Winter Cow: descend" with an arrow pointing down. "I guess I'll have to descend," you say.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You <kanji>descend</kanji> to the bottom and there it is, the Winter Cow. You walk up to the cow, and it swings its head back to see you. Instead of a cow's face, it's <reading>こう</reading>いち's face.
kanji.readingHint: "Hello, thank you for descending all the way down here to see me," says the Winter Cow with こういち's face. "You thought you could use the Winter Cow to stop me, but as you can see, I got here first."

rawData.meaning_hint: Focus on descending the stairs. There are many signs that say "To Winter Cow: descend" with an arrow pointing down. "I guess I'll have to descend," you say.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The <radical>clothes</radical> radical and the <kanji>clothes</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Surprising!
kanji.readingMnemonic: An <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) sees a pile of your <kanji>clothes</kanji> and lays an <reading>e</reading>gg (え) in them. The eagle’s egg needs a soft warm place to incubate, and your pile of clothes is the perfect place.
kanji.readingHint: Look at the eagle and her egg sitting atop your clothes. Every time you get close the eagle starts to screech at you fiercely. Screee! Screee!

rawData.reading_hint: Look at the eagle and her egg sitting atop your clothes. Every time you get close the eagle starts to screech at you fiercely. Screee! Screee!

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
When it comes to <kanji>build</kanji>ing and <kanji>construct</kanji>ing, the thing that encompasses both of them is <vocabulary>architecture</vocabulary>.

建築 refers to the design and <vocabulary>construction</vocabulary> of buildings. Occasionally it can also refer to a physical building itself, but for that it's more common to use 建築物.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

rawData.meaning_mnemonic:
When it comes to <kanji>build</kanji>ing and <kanji>construct</kanji>ing, the thing that encompasses both of them is <vocabulary>architecture</vocabulary>.

建築 refers to the design and <vocabulary>construction</vocabulary> of buildings. Occasionally it can also refer to a physical building itself, but for that it's more common to use 建築物.
rawData.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

context_sentences.en: This town is famous for its unique architecture.
context_sentences.ja: この町はユニークな建築で有名です。

context_sentences.en: That building has become a landmark of the city.
context_sentences.ja: その建築物は市のランドマークになっています。

context_sentences.en: I am studying architecture at university.
context_sentences.ja: 私は大学で建築学を学んでいます。

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: Something that's <kanji>new</kanji>ly <kanji>construct</kanji>ed is something that's <vocabulary>newly built</vocabulary>.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

context_sentences.en: I don't want to live anywhere other than a new apartment building.
context_sentences.ja: 新築のマンション以外には住みたくありません。

context_sentences.en: I've heard that newly built homes tend to cause more allergies, but is that true?
context_sentences.ja: 新築の方がアレルギーが出ることが多いって聞いたんですけど、本当ですか？

context_sentences.en: Buying a newly built house will cheer you up.
context_sentences.ja: 新築の家でも買えば、気分が晴れるよ。

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: <kanji> Congratulations</kanji> on your <kanji>condition</kanji>. I'll send you a <vocabulary>greeting card</vocabulary> to make it official. Though this kind of card is almost always in the form of a <vocabulary>New Year's Card</vocabulary>.

context_sentences.en: I sent a congratulations card to my nephew when he passed his high school entrance exam.
